acceptance_targets: [CH04_CRAFT_CONTINUITY_FIRE_EXTINGUISHER] Pass if snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:125, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:141, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151 no longer depict a fire extinguisher as a carried, raised, or blood-marked weapon in chapter_04. Fail if any fire-extinguisher combat beat remains on the page.

[CH04_CRAFT_OPERATIONAL_LEGIBILITY_ASSAULT] Pass if a cold reader can finish snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:123 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151 and answer four questions without inference from later chapters: what Raul's group is trying to reach or damage, why the blockers are stationed there, what action turns the standoff lethal, and how that action relates to the dead man's switch. Fail if any of those four points still has to be guessed.

[CH04_DIALOGUE_VIKTOR_CALIBRATION_ECHO] Pass if the revised speech at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11 no longer tracks the calibration line's 'proposal / not because the work matters anymore / structure gets us through' shape or a close synonym version of it, while still sounding recognizably like Viktor and using at least one contraction consistent with his stressed moderate-contraction register. Fail if the line still reads as a near-paraphrase of the example.

[CH04_PROSE_OVERSTATEMENT_AFTER_ARRIVAL] Pass if the paragraphs beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:109, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:171 each add a new physical, social, or tactical change on the page and do not classify the deaths by type or order or restate Ember's faction-breaking strategy in abstract terms. Fail if the revision still contains summary beats equivalent to 'That's the new thing,' 'The first was...,' or 'the truth turned out to be a solvent.'

[CH04_PROSE_REPEATED_SOMEONE_WHO_SCAFFOLD] Pass if, across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:49, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:127, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:129, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:137, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:165, no more than one 'someone who...' explanatory scaffold remains and at least three of those beats now render character state through observable action or concrete description. Fail if the repeated template still appears in multiple cited locations.
[F08] No named character's death is rendered solely through body discovery. At minimum, the dying process (final breaths, loss of consciousness, physical shutdown) must be present on the page.
[F19] Every death rendered in more than one sentence identifies the victim by name.
[R033] Ch4 contains at most 2 uses of 'fluorescent.'
[R044] Chapter 04 contains em-dashes on no more than 6 prose lines (≈8%).
[C009] Priya does not possess a phone in Ch4, OR the text explains how she retained/recovered one.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:125, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:141, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151 no longer depict a fire extinguisher as a carried, raised, or blood-marked weapon in chapter_04. Fail if any fire-extinguisher combat beat remains on the page.
findings.problem: Chapter 04 weaponizes a fire extinguisher even though the continuity sheet tracks the fire extinguisher as a later escalation object introduced and first weaponized in chapter_07. Pulling one from the wall here, raising it in the fight, and leaving it blood-spotted on the tile creates an object chronology contradiction and weakens the later escalation beat.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the weapon handling at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:125, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:141, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151 so Raul's group carries and abandons only tools that are continuity-neutral in this chapter, such as the already-present pipe or pry bar, or otherwise remove the fire-extinguisher combat beat entirely so chapter_07 remains the first fire-extinguisher weaponization.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold reader can finish snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:123 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151 and answer four questions without inference from later chapters: what Raul's group is trying to reach or damage, why the blockers are stationed there, what action turns the standoff lethal, and how that action relates to the dead man's switch. Fail if any of those four points still has to be guessed.
findings.problem: The first-floor clash is the chapter's major tactical turn, but the operative objective and resistance chain stay blurry on the page. Noor can only infer Raul was headed for 'the server room, the loading dock, somewhere with hardware,' and the scene leans on a later summary to tell the reader the dead man was stopping a computer-destruction attempt. Because this confrontation hinges on technical action around the dead man's switch, the local cause-and-effect is under-rendered.
findings.rewrite_direction: Clarify the assault logic directly inside snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:123, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:125, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:131, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:145: name which room or hardware Raul is trying to reach or damage, why these three people are physically blocking that corridor right now, and what specific move would trigger the switch or change everyone's survival odds before the summary paragraph at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151 arrives.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised speech at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11 no longer tracks the calibration line's 'proposal / not because the work matters anymore / structure gets us through' shape or a close synonym version of it, while still sounding recognizably like Viktor and using at least one contraction consistent with his stressed moderate-contraction register. Fail if the line still reads as a near-paraphrase of the example.
findings.problem: Viktor's opening speech reads as a near-paraphrase of a calibration-only style-bible example: the same proposal frame, the same 'not because the work matters anymore' pivot, and the same argument that structure will get them through. On a chapter-defining beat, that damages voice integrity and makes the line feel derived rather than spoken fresh from this scene.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite the meeting-opening speech at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:11 so Viktor still sounds committee-trained, procedural, and stressed, but does not reuse the calibration example's sentence contour or synonym chain. Give him fresh institutional vocabulary, his own qualifying sprawl, and a distinct turn into the watch-rotation idea.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the paragraphs beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:109, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:171 each add a new physical, social, or tactical change on the page and do not classify the deaths by type or order or restate Ember's faction-breaking strategy in abstract terms. Fail if the revision still contains summary beats equivalent to 'That's the new thing,' 'The first was...,' or 'the truth turned out to be a solvent.'
findings.problem: After Yuki's body and the corridor killing land in concrete images, the narration keeps translating them into thesis language instead of letting the new pressure stand. 'That's the new thing,' the four-deaths taxonomy, and the closing 'truth turned out to be a solvent' explanation all re-prove meanings the scene has already made legible, which thins dread into commentary.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:109, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:151, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:171, replace the taxonomic summary with new on-page consequence: a failed search, a changed alignment, bodily residue, a specific accusation, or another concrete pressure move. Keep Noor's analytic lens, but make the analysis arise from what she notices rather than from numbered classifications or thesis phrasing that culminates again at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:175.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:49, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:127, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:129, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:137, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:165, no more than one 'someone who...' explanatory scaffold remains and at least three of those beats now render character state through observable action or concrete description. Fail if the repeated template still appears in multiple cited locations.
findings.problem: The chapter repeatedly leans on the same explanatory scaffold - a quality or posture defined as belonging to 'someone who...' - until it starts reading like autopilot rather than observation. The repetition is marked enough to flatten several otherwise good character beats into one reusable sentence template.
findings.rewrite_direction: Vary or replace the repeated scaffold at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:49, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:127, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:129, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:137, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_04.md:165. Keep at most one of these constructions if needed; let the other beats land through concrete gesture, rhythm, or sensory detail instead of explanatory template language.

findings.acceptance_test: No named character's death is rendered solely through body discovery. At minimum, the dying process (final breaths, loss of consciousness, physical shutdown) must be present on the page.
findings.evidence: Lines 696-730: 'Yuki Kim is on the concrete landing between flights... There's a wound in her right side below the ribs, and a second wound higher, near the collarbone.' Aftermath only; no witness to the act.
findings.problem: Yuki Kim's death happens entirely off-page. Noor discovers the body in the stairwell with two stab wounds described in aftermath, but the act itself is never witnessed. The style bible says 'Do not cut away from a death scene.' This is the first anonymous killing and it sets a precedent for later off-page deaths.
findings.rewrite_direction: Have Noor hear the sounds from the stairwell (truncated cry, the wet sound of blade entry, running footsteps) and arrive during Yuki's final breaths. The killer can remain anonymous, but the dying should be on the page.

findings.acceptance_test: Every death rendered in more than one sentence identifies the victim by name.
findings.evidence: Lines 752-766: The victim is physically described but never named. Noor's inability to recall his name is narrated.
findings.problem: Corridor fight victim is deliberately unnamed ('whose name Noor should know and can't retrieve'). This is a thematic choice about dehumanization through accumulating death, but the namelessness reduces specificity and functions as a distancing mechanism.
findings.rewrite_direction: Name the man. Let the name sit on the page so the reader carries it. The dehumanization theme is better served by naming victims and letting the reader feel the accumulation than by the narrator performing forgetting.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch4 contains at most 2 uses of 'fluorescent.'
findings.evidence: The word 'fluorescent' appears 72 times across the manuscript. Ch4 has 7 instances (highest). Keep 8-10 across the full novel; Ch4 should retain at most 2.
findings.problem: 'Fluorescent' at 72 occurrences (0.91 per 1000 prose words) is the most overused sensory image in the manuscript. Ch4 (7 instances in 4,226 words) is the densest chapter. Target: reduce manuscript-wide to 8-10 instances.
findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch4, keep the 2 strongest fluorescent references (suggest the ones tied to specific emotional or plot beats). Cut the remaining 5 instances. Replace with varied lighting vocabulary or cut the lighting reference entirely where it serves no function.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 04 contains em-dashes on no more than 6 prose lines (≈8%).
findings.problem: Chapter 04 has 52.3% em-dash density (45 of 86 prose lines contain em-dashes), far above the 10% threshold. Manuscript-wide density is 43.2%.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines to ~6 (≈8% of 86 prose lines). Convert most parenthetical asides to commas or separate sentences. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest interjections or corrections.

findings.acceptance_test: Priya does not possess a phone in Ch4, OR the text explains how she retained/recovered one.
findings.evidence: Ch1 line 13: 'no personal devices past the secure perimeter... collecting phones with the practiced neutrality of someone who does this at the beginning of every sprint.' Ch4 line 688 (Day 3): 'Her phone — useless for calls, useless for messages — is face-down in her lap.'
findings.problem: All personal devices were collected at the security barrier on Day 1 (Ch1:13). Priya should not have a phone on Day 3 (Ch4:688). No scene shows her retaining or recovering it.
findings.rewrite_direction: Either remove the phone from Ch4 line 688, or add an earlier note explaining how Priya retained it (e.g., a second device, or retrieved from the collection bin).

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
